response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As someone who doesn't use social media at all, I have a unique perspective on this topic. I see it as an interesting example of how technology can sometimes overshadow common sense in our daily lives. Having been raised without much formal education but surrounded by practical mechanics and family values, I've learned to approach information with skepticism long before the internet was big. Managing fake content is really about maintaining that skeptical mindset you'd need when tinkering under a car or fixing pipes at home. You don't trust everything until it's verified through hands-on experience.\n\nNowadays, people jump into social media without thinking critically about what they're seeing. It reminds me of my kids playing with their phones – they just absorb whatever comes on the screen like sponges after bath time. I'm not talking from authority here; my education stopped at primary school teaching basic math and reading, but that's enough to know that fake things exist everywhere in life.\n\nSo dealing with it is simple: ignore most of it unless it directly affects your work or family. My wife understands this perfectly because our household runs on practical priorities over digital distractions.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I started from a background where misinformation isn't something abstracted by screens but tangible like faulty wiring.\n\n1) Since I don't engage with social media, my opinion comes directly from lived experience rather than online trends.\n2) The traits of being low openness and low conscientiousness actually align well with this topic because it's about straightforward solutions to complex problems – much like diagnosing a car engine issue through simple observation.",
  "tone": "Pragmatic",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As someone who doesn't use social media at all, I have a unique perspective on this topic. I see it as an interesting example of how technology can sometimes overshadow common sense in our daily lives. Having been raised without much formal education but surrounded by practical mechanics and family values, I've learned to approach information with skepticism long before the internet was big. Managing fake content is really about maintaining that skeptical mindset you'd need when tinkering under a car or fixing pipes at home. You don't trust everything until it's verified through hands-on experience.

Nowadays, people jump into social media without thinking critically about what they're seeing. It reminds me of my kids playing with their phones – they just absorb whatever comes on the screen like sponges after bath time. I'm not talking from authority here; my education stopped at primary school teaching basic math and reading, but that's enough to know that fake things exist everywhere in life.

So dealing with it is simple: ignore most of it unless it directly affects your work or family. My wife understands this perfectly because our household runs on practical priorities over digital distractions.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started from a background where misinformation isn't something abstracted by screens but tangible like faulty wiring.

1) Since I don't engage with social media, my opinion comes directly from lived experience rather than online trends.
2) The traits of being low openness and low conscientiousness actually align well with this topic because it's about straightforward solutions to complex problems – much like diagnosing a car engine issue through simple observation.
